name: Noise Meter
description: A player- or AI-facing model of how much sound the player generates, how far it propagates, and which agents can hear it.
design_notes: Noise meter turns movement, surfaces, actions, and equipment into AI hearing signals. Define noise_event_id generation, surface material modifiers, crouch multipliers, occlusion policy, AI hearing thresholds, UI display, and decay. The meter should teach players how sound affects stealth without requiring them to memorize hidden numbers.
design_purpose: Make sound a readable stealth resource alongside visibility and cover.
Let players trade pace, stance, surface choice, and action noise against AI hearing risk.
Give designers a debug-friendly way to tune detection without changing every AI individually.
player_fantasy: The player feels tense and deliberate, listening to their own actions and choosing when silence matters.

parameters.name: noise_loudness_db
parameters.description: Sound loudness assigned to the current player action.

parameters.name: noise_radius_units
parameters.description: World-space propagation radius for AI hearing tests.

parameters.name: surface_material_noise_multiplier
parameters.description: Modifier from grass, metal, water, glass, or carpet surfaces.

parameters.name: crouch_noise_multiplier
parameters.description: Noise modifier applied while crouched or otherwise stealth-walking.

parameters.name: ai_hearing_threshold_db
parameters.description: Minimum loudness required before an AI agent reacts.

parameters.name: meter_decay_points_per_second
parameters.description: UI decay used after the latest noise event.

edge_cases: A loud action in another room should account for sound_occlusion_policy_id instead of alerting every nearby AI equally.
Stacked actions such as sprinting while reloading need a rule for combining or prioritizing noise events.
Moving platforms and vehicles may contribute their own noise source separate from player footsteps.
Checkpoint restore should clear transient noise events but preserve persistent alarms or broken glass.
common_bugs: Noise UI displays raw loudness while AI uses occluded loudness, making player feedback misleading.
Surface material lookup samples the wrong foot position and changes loudness every step unpredictably.
Crouch noise multiplier stacks repeatedly after stance transitions.
AI hearing checks use render frames and miss short noise events at low frame rates.
balancing_notes: High surface contrast makes route choice meaningful; low contrast turns noise into a decorative meter.
Hearing thresholds should be tuned with patrol spacing and ambient audio cover, not only action loudness.
Showing exact radius is powerful assistance; hiding it creates more uncertainty and tension.
accessibility_notes: Provide visual noise indicators and subtitles or icons for sound-based AI reactions.
Avoid relying only on stereo audio direction when noise placement is part of stealth problem solving.

implementation_notes.unity: Emit noise_event_id through a central perception service with loudness, radius, surface_material_id, and source position. AI agents query occluded loudness in fixed AI ticks, while UI reads the same resolved event data for consistency.
implementation_notes.godot: Route player actions through a noise bus that records loudness, radius, and material multiplier. Navigation or physics queries can apply wall occlusion before AI blackboards receive hearing events.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use AI Perception hearing stimuli or Gameplay Tags for action categories. Server-side AI should own hearing decisions, and debug drawing should show noise radius, occlusion hits, and receiving agents.
implementation_notes.web: Store noise events with source id, loudness, radius, and expiry tick. A spatial query finds candidate listeners, applies surface and occlusion modifiers, then writes perception_event_id into AI decision state.

relationships.reason: Noise meter output supplies loudness and radius data that AI investigation can consume.
Crouch movement can reduce noise output through stance multipliers.
Non-audio indicators can expose noise state for players who cannot rely on sound cues.

example_games.title: Thief: The Dark Project
example_games.note: Uses surface sound and movement noise as stealth concerns.

example_games.title: Mark of the Ninja
example_games.note: Uses readable sound indicators for stealth planning.
